activities.activity_description: How much do you really know about human trafficking? Read each statement and decide: Myth or Fact?

questions.question_text: Sex trafficking is the most common and widespread type of human trafficking.

answer_options.feedback_text: Both sex trafficking and labor trafficking are common. It is difficult to say which is more widespread because statistics and data are difficult to gather, and some existing research is limited.
Both sex trafficking and labor trafficking are common. It is difficult to say which is more widespread because statistics and data are difficult to gather, and some existing research is limited.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right! Both types are real and serious. Don't let movies fool you!
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Common myth! Labor trafficking is just as real — and often invisible.

questions.question_text: Perpetrators of sex trafficking often exploit people they already know.

answer_options.feedback_text: Perpetrators often target people they know — family members, friends, romantic partners, and acquaintances. Traffickers who begin as strangers will usually form some type of relationship to earn trust before attempting to traffic them.

Perpetrators often target people they know — family members, friends, romantic partners, and acquaintances. Traffickers who begin as strangers will usually form some type of relationship to earn trust before attempting to traffic them.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Exactly. Traffickers build trust first. That's what makes it so hard to spot.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, traffickers very often know their victims — that's part of what makes grooming so dangerous.

questions.question_text: People who experience trafficking are usually kidnapped or physically confined by their traffickers.

answer_options.feedback_text: Survivors are often not physically confined. Traffickers use psychological manipulation, emotional control, debt, and fear instead. Many survivors may not even identify themselves as victims because the relationship feels complicated.

Survivors are often not physically confined. Traffickers use psychological manipulation, emotional control, debt, and fear instead. Many survivors may not even identify themselves as victims because the relationship feels complicated.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Yes! Control is usually psychological — not physical chains.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Very common misunderstanding. The control is mostly psychological.

questions.question_text: Only girls and women can be victims of sex trafficking.

answer_options.feedback_text: Men and boys can also experience sex trafficking. While we don't know exactly what percentage of victims identify as male, we do know that male victims are less likely to be identified. Anyone, regardless of gender, can be targeted.

Men and boys can also experience sex trafficking. While we don't know exactly what percentage of victims identify as male, we do know that male victims are less likely to be identified. Anyone, regardless of gender, can be targeted.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right — anyone can be targeted. Gender doesn't protect you.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: This is a harmful myth. Male victims exist and are often overlooked.

questions.question_text: Labor trafficking is a global issue that also occurs inside the United States.

answer_options.feedback_text: Labor trafficking happens in the U.S. across many industries including agriculture, construction, restaurants, nail salons, and domestic work. It is not only a problem in other countries.
Labor trafficking happens in the U.S. across many industries including agriculture, construction, restaurants, nail salons, and domestic work. It is not only a problem in other countries.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: 100% fact. It happens right here, often in plain sight.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Surprise — labor trafficking does happen in the U.S. across many industries.

questions.question_text: Human trafficking and human smuggling are the same crime.

answer_options.feedback_text: Human smuggling involves illegally transporting people across international borders. Human trafficking is about exploitation using force, fraud, or coercion — it doesn't require any movement across borders at all.

Human smuggling involves illegally transporting people across international borders. Human trafficking is about exploitation using force, fraud, or coercion — it doesn't require any movement across borders at all.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Good catch! Smuggling = crossing borders. Trafficking = exploitation.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: These are two different crimes. Trafficking doesn't even require moving someone.

questions.question_text: Traffickers prey on vulnerabilities, not specific demographics — anyone can be targeted.

answer_options.feedback_text: Traffickers look for vulnerabilities like isolation, financial hardship, family problems, or emotional distress. While some groups face higher risk, trafficking can happen to anyone. Awareness and protective factors help reduce risk for everyone.

Traffickers look for vulnerabilities like isolation, financial hardship, family problems, or emotional distress. While some groups face higher risk, trafficking can happen to anyone. Awareness and protective factors help reduce risk for everyone.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Exactly right. Vulnerability is the target — not age, race, or zip code.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, traffickers look for vulnerabilities, not a specific type of person.

questions.question_text: People who are being trafficked always want help getting out immediately.

answer_options.feedback_text: People in trafficking situations may not identify as victims, may feel loyal to their trafficker due to grooming, or may fear what happens if they leave. Emotional manipulation, shame, and fear of law enforcement are common reasons survivors don't seek help right away.

People in trafficking situations may not identify as victims, may feel loyal to their trafficker due to grooming, or may fear what happens if they leave. Emotional manipulation, shame, and fear of law enforcement are common reasons survivors don't seek help right away.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Exactly — it's complicated. Emotional manipulation makes leaving really hard.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: This is a misconception. Trauma bonds and fear make it complicated.

questions.question_text: Labor trafficking can only happen in agriculture or farming industries.

answer_options.feedback_text: Labor trafficking can occur in any industry involving forced labor — construction, restaurants, nail salons, domestic work, traveling sales crews, technology, and more. Coercive work environments with wage theft, threats, and restricted movement are red flags in any sector.

Labor trafficking can occur in any industry involving forced labor — construction, restaurants, nail salons, domestic work, traveling sales crews, technology, and more. Coercive work environments with wage theft, threats, and restricted movement are red flags in any sector.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right! It can happen in restaurants, nail salons, tech — anywhere.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Nope! Labor trafficking happens across many industries, including ones nearby.

questions.question_text: Sex trafficking of a minor does not require proof of force, fraud, or coercion.

answer_options.feedback_text: Under federal law (the Trafficking Victims Protection Act), if a minor is induced to perform a commercial sex act, it is sex trafficking regardless of whether force, fraud, or coercion was used. There is no such thing as a 'child prostitute' — a minor in this situation is always a victim of trafficking.

Under federal law (the Trafficking Victims Protection Act), if a minor is induced to perform a commercial sex act, it is sex trafficking regardless of whether force, fraud, or coercion was used. There is no such thing as a 'child prostitute' — a minor in this situation is always a victim of trafficking.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Important legal fact. Minors cannot consent to commercial sex — period.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Under federal law, minors are automatically victims — no need to prove force.

questions.question_text: A warning going around online says traffickers tie zip ties to car door handles in parking lots to mark vehicles belonging to potential victims.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is a myth. Polaris reviewed National Human Trafficking Hotline data from 2015 to 2022 and found zero cases involving zip ties used this way. Viral warnings like this can distract people from real warning signs and pull attention away from the resources meant to help actual victims.

This is a myth. Polaris reviewed National Human Trafficking Hotline data from 2015 to 2022 and found zero cases involving zip ties used this way. Viral warnings like this can distract people from real warning signs and pull attention away from the resources meant to help actual victims.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right — this one's been circulating online for years, but the data just doesn't back it up.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this is a myth. There's no evidence traffickers use zip ties to mark targets.

questions.question_text: Another viral post warns that traffickers hand out drug-laced flowers or roses to women in parking lots in order to drug and abduct them.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is a myth. Polaris checked the same hotline data and found zero cases matching this story either. It's been debunked since at least 2019 but keeps resurfacing. Spreading unverified rumors like this can overwhelm services meant for real victims.

This is a myth. Polaris checked the same hotline data and found zero cases matching this story either. It's been debunked since at least 2019 but keeps resurfacing. Spreading unverified rumors like this can overwhelm services meant for real victims.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Exactly — another one that sounds plausible but doesn't hold up.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this is a myth too — no documented cases match this story.

activities.activity_description: Healthy relationships are built on respect for boundaries. Read each scenario and decide: is this Safe, or is it Sus?

questions.question_text: Bailey and Cody have been dating for a few weeks. Cody keeps asking Bailey to send revealing photos of herself. Bailey has said no and changed the subject several times, but Cody keeps pushing, saying it would make them closer.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is sus. Bailey has already said no, more than once. Repeated pressure after a boundary has been set isn't okay, no matter how it's framed. This touches digital, physical, and emotional boundaries all at once. Sharing explicit images between minors is also illegal in most states, even if both people agree to it.

This is sus. Bailey has already said no, more than once. Repeated pressure after a boundary has been set isn't okay, no matter how it's framed. This touches digital, physical, and emotional boundaries all at once. Sharing explicit images between minors is also illegal in most states, even if both people agree to it.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Exactly — a 'no' that keeps getting ignored is a red flag, not romance.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this one's sus. Repeated pressure after a 'no' is a boundary violation.

questions.question_text: Parker asked Noor out, and she said no but offered to hang out in group settings. Parker keeps asking her out anyway, sometimes in front of friends, and tags her in social media posts hoping she'll respond. Noor has started avoiding events where Parker might be.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is sus. Noor set a clear boundary, and even if Parker doesn't mean any harm, repeatedly asking again — especially in public or online — ignores what she said. It's an emotional boundary issue, and could become a physical one if Parker keeps not taking 'no' for an answer.

This is sus. Noor set a clear boundary, and even if Parker doesn't mean any harm, repeatedly asking again — especially in public or online — ignores what she said. It's an emotional boundary issue, and could become a physical one if Parker keeps not taking 'no' for an answer.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right — good intentions don't cancel out ignoring someone's 'no.'
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this is sus. Persisting after a clear 'no' crosses a boundary, even unintentionally.

questions.question_text: Priya made the jazz band and now has less free time for her best friend Sophie. When Priya's new bandmates invite her to lunch, she asks if Sophie can join too, so Sophie won't feel left out.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is safe! Priya is balancing her new interests with her existing friendship, and including Sophie shows she values that relationship. Healthy relationships can grow to include new people without anyone being pushed out.

This is safe! Priya is balancing her new interests with her existing friendship, and including Sophie shows she values that relationship. Healthy relationships can grow to include new people without anyone being pushed out.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Yep — making room for old friends in new spaces is a healthy move.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this one's safe. Priya is including her friend, not pushing her out.

questions.question_text: Avery is part of the LGBTQ+ community and sometimes deals with unsupportive comments from family. Avery talks to online friends about it for support. One of those friends suggests Avery run away from home, and even suggests responding to the unkind family member with violence.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is sus. Even if the friend means well, suggesting Avery run away or respond with violence could put Avery in real danger. This is a digital boundary situation — it's okay to tell a friend that their advice doesn't feel safe, even while appreciating that they care.

This is sus. Even if the friend means well, suggesting Avery run away or respond with violence could put Avery in real danger. This is a digital boundary situation — it's okay to tell a friend that their advice doesn't feel safe, even while appreciating that they care.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right. Advice that could put someone in more danger is sus, even from a friend.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this is sus. That advice could put Avery in more danger, not less.

questions.question_text: Jay joined the soccer team and started spending more time with teammates after practice. His girlfriend Madison got upset and demanded access to his phone, threatening to break up with him if he said no. He gave in — now she regularly checks his phone and shows up unannounced wherever he is.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is sus. Threatening to end a relationship to force someone to give up their privacy is a form of manipulation. Demanding phone access and showing up uninvited crosses both digital and emotional boundaries — trust shouldn't require surveillance.

This is sus. Threatening to end a relationship to force someone to give up their privacy is a form of manipulation. Demanding phone access and showing up uninvited crosses both digital and emotional boundaries — trust shouldn't require surveillance.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Exactly — 'prove it or I'll leave' is pressure, not trust.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this is sus. Using a relationship as leverage to demand privacy access is a red flag.

questions.question_text: Mike's supervisor Elsa praised his work and asked him to also take shifts at her other gas station, totaling about 65 hours a week. His paystub shows no overtime pay, he rarely gets breaks, and Elsa told him not to mention the extra hours because his coworkers might get jealous.

answer_options.feedback_text: This is sus. Not paying required overtime, denying breaks, and asking an employee to keep quiet about it are signs of labor exploitation — a physical and emotional boundary violation. Wage theft and break violations can be reported to the Department of Labor or local law enforcement.

This is sus. Not paying required overtime, denying breaks, and asking an employee to keep quiet about it are signs of labor exploitation — a physical and emotional boundary violation. Wage theft and break violations can be reported to the Department of Labor or local law enforcement.

questions.avatar_speech_correct: Right — unpaid overtime plus 'don't tell anyone' is a major warning sign at work.
questions.avatar_speech_wrong: Actually, this is sus. No overtime pay, no breaks, and 'keep it quiet' are workplace red flags.
